subject: Mathematics
question_text: Write an anti derivative of function cos 2x using the method of inspection.
question_html: Write an anti derivative of function cos 2x using the method of inspection.
solution_text: A function whose derivative is cos 2x. {tex}\frac{d}{d x} \sin 2 x=2 \cos 2 x{/tex} or {tex}\cos 2 x=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d}{d x}(\sin 2 x)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x\right){/tex} Therefore, an anti derivative of cos 2x is {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x{/tex}
solution_html:
A function whose derivative is cos 2x.
{tex}\frac{d}{d x} \sin 2 x=2 \cos 2 x{/tex} 
or {tex}\cos 2 x=\frac{1}{2} \frac{d}{d x}(\sin 2 x)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x\right){/tex} 
Therefore, an anti derivative of cos 2x is {tex}\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Write an anti derivative of function using the method of inspection: 3x 2 + 4x 3
question_html: Write an anti derivative of function using the method of inspection: 3x2 + 4x3
solution_text: A function whose anti derivative is 3x 2 + 4x 3 . {tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{3}+x^{4}\right)=3 x^{2}+4 x^{3}{/tex} Therefore, an anti derivative of 3x 2 + 4x 3 is x 3 + x 4
solution_html:
A function whose anti derivative is 3x2 + 4x3.
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}\left(x^{3}+x^{4}\right)=3 x^{2}+4 x^{3}{/tex} 
Therefore, an anti derivative of 3x2 + 4x3 is x3 + x4

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Write an anti derivative of function using the method of inspection: {tex}\frac{1}{x}, x \neq 0{/tex}
question_html: Write an anti derivative of function using the method of inspection: {tex}\frac{1}{x}, x \neq 0{/tex}
solution_text: We know that {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log x)=\frac{1}{x}, x>0{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d}{d x}[\log (-x)]=\frac{1}{-x}(-1)=\frac{1}{x}, x<0{/tex} Combining above, we get {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log |x|)=\frac{1}{x}, x \neq 0{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{1}{x} d x=\log |x|{/tex} is one of the anti derivatives of {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that
{tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log x)=\frac{1}{x}, x>0{/tex} and {tex}\frac{d}{d x}[\log (-x)]=\frac{1}{-x}(-1)=\frac{1}{x}, x<0{/tex} 
Combining above, we get {tex}\frac{d}{d x}(\log |x|)=\frac{1}{x}, x \neq 0{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{1}{x} d x=\log |x|{/tex} is one of the anti derivatives of {tex}\frac{1}{x}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: We have {tex}\int \frac{x^{3}-1}{x^{2}} d x=\int x d x-\int x^{-2} d x{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{x^{1+1}}{1+1}+C_{1}\right)-\left(\frac{x^{-2+1}}{-2+1}+C_{2}\right){/tex} ; C 1 , C 2 are constants of integration = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+C_{1}-\frac{x^{-1}}{-1}-C_{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{x}+C_{1}-C_{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{x}+C{/tex} where C = C 1 - C 2 is another constant of integration
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\int \frac{x^{3}-1}{x^{2}} d x=\int x d x-\int x^{-2} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\left(\frac{x^{1+1}}{1+1}+C_{1}\right)-\left(\frac{x^{-2+1}}{-2+1}+C_{2}\right){/tex}; C1, C2 are constants of integration
= {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+C_{1}-\frac{x^{-1}}{-1}-C_{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{x}+C_{1}-C_{2}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{2}+\frac{1}{x}+C{/tex} where C = C1 - C2 is another constant of integration

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: We have {tex}\int\left(x^{\frac{2}{3}}+1\right) d x=\int x^{\frac{2}{3}} d x+\int 1d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{\frac{2}{3}+1}}{\frac{2}{3}+1}+x+\mathrm{C}=\frac{3}{5} x^{\frac{5}{3}}+x+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\int\left(x^{\frac{2}{3}}+1\right) d x=\int x^{\frac{2}{3}} d x+\int 1d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x^{\frac{2}{3}+1}}{\frac{2}{3}+1}+x+\mathrm{C}=\frac{3}{5} x^{\frac{5}{3}}+x+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}+2 e^{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right) d x{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}\int\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}+2 e^{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right) d x=\int x^{\frac{3}{2}} d x+\int 2 e^{x} d x-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}+1}}{\frac{3}{2}+1}+2 e^{x}-\log |x|+C{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{5} x^{\frac{5}{2}}+2 e^{x}-\log |x|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\int\left(x^{\frac{3}{2}}+2 e^{x}-\frac{1}{x}\right) d x=\int x^{\frac{3}{2}} d x+\int 2 e^{x} d x-\int \frac{1}{x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x^{\frac{3}{2}+1}}{\frac{3}{2}+1}+2 e^{x}-\log |x|+C{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2}{5} x^{\frac{5}{2}}+2 e^{x}-\log |x|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int(\sin x+\cos x) d x{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int(\sin x+\cos x) d x{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}\int(\sin x+\cos x) d x=\int \sin x d x+\int \cos x d x{/tex} = -cos x + sin x + C
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\int(\sin x+\cos x) d x=\int \sin x d x+\int \cos x d x{/tex} 
= -cos x + sin x + C

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int cosec x(cosec x+\cot x) d x{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int cosec x(cosec x+\cot x) d x{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}\int\left(cosec x(cosec x+\cot x) d x=\int cosec ^{2} x d x+\int cosec x \cot x d x\right.{/tex} = -cot x - cosec x + C
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\int\left(cosec x(cosec x+\cot x) d x=\int cosec ^{2} x d x+\int cosec x \cot x d x\right.{/tex} 
= -cot x - cosec x + C

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{1-\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{1-\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}\int \frac{1-\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x} d x=\int \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x} d x-\int \frac{\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sec ^{2} x d x-\int \tan x \sec x d x{/tex} = tan x - sec x + C
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\int \frac{1-\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x} d x=\int \frac{1}{\cos ^{2} x} d x-\int \frac{\sin x}{\cos ^{2} x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\int \sec ^{2} x d x-\int \tan x \sec x d x{/tex} 
= tan x - sec x + C

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the anti derivative F of f defined by f(x) = 4x 3 - 6, where F(0) = 3
question_html: Find the anti derivative F of f defined by f(x) = 4x3 - 6, where F(0) = 3
solution_text: Here, f(x) = x 4 - 6x Anti derivative F of f(x) is given by; {tex}F(x)=\int f(x) d x{/tex} {tex}=\int\left(4 x^{3}-6\right) d x{/tex} {tex}=x^{4}-6 x+C{/tex} {tex}\text { So, } F(x)=x^{4}-6 x+C, {/tex} where C is constant of Integration. Given: F(0) = 3, which gives, 3 = 0 - 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + C {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 3 Hence, the required anti derivative is the unique function F defined by F(x) = x 4 - 6x + 3
solution_html:
Here, f(x) = x4 - 6x
Anti derivative F of f(x) is given by;
{tex}F(x)=\int f(x) d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int\left(4 x^{3}-6\right) d x{/tex}
{tex}=x^{4}-6 x+C{/tex}
{tex}\text { So, } F(x)=x^{4}-6 x+C, {/tex}  where C is constant of Integration.
Given: F(0) = 3, which gives,
3 = 0 - 6 {tex}\times{/tex} 0 + C
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 3
Hence, the required anti derivative is the unique function F defined by F(x) = x4 - 6x + 3

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Integrate the function sin mx w.r.t. x.
solution_text: We know that derivative of mx is m. Thus, we make the substitution mx = t so that mdx = dt. Therefore, {tex}\int \sin m x d x=\frac{1}{m} \int \sin t d t{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{m} \cos t+\mathrm{C}=-\frac{1}{m} \cos m x+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that derivative of mx is m. 
Thus, we make the substitution mx = t so that mdx = dt.
Therefore, {tex}\int \sin m x d x=\frac{1}{m} \int \sin t d t{/tex} {tex}=-\frac{1}{m} \cos t+\mathrm{C}=-\frac{1}{m} \cos m x+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Integrate the function w.r.t. x: 2x sin (x 2 + 1)
solution_text: Derivative of x 2 + 1 is 2x. Thus, we use the substitution x 2 + 1 = t so that 2x dx = dt. Therefore, {tex}\int 2 x \sin \left(x^{2}+1\right) d x=\int \sin t d t=-\cos t+C=-\cos \left(x^{2}+1\right)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Derivative of x2 + 1 is 2x. Thus, we use the substitution x2 + 1 = t so that 2x dx = dt.
Therefore, {tex}\int 2 x \sin \left(x^{2}+1\right) d x=\int \sin t d t=-\cos t+C=-\cos \left(x^{2}+1\right)+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Derivative of {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{1}{2} x^{-\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex} . Thus, we use the substitution {tex}\sqrt{x}=t{/tex} so that {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} d x=d t{/tex} giving dx = 2t dt. Thus, {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{4} \sqrt{x} \sec ^{2} \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{2 t \tan ^{4} t \sec ^{2} t d t}{t}{/tex} = {tex}2 \int \tan ^{4} t \sec ^{2} t d t{/tex} Again, we make another substitution tan t = u so that sec 2 t dt = du Therefore, {tex}2 \int \tan ^{4} t \sec ^{2} t d t=2 \int u^{4} d u=2 \frac{u^{5}}{5}+C{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{5} \tan ^{5} t+C{/tex} (since u = tan t) = {tex}\frac{2}{5} \tan ^{5} \sqrt{x}+C{/tex} (since {tex}t=\sqrt{x}{/tex} ) Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{4} \sqrt{x} \sec ^{2} \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}} d x=\frac{2}{5} \tan ^{5} \sqrt{x}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Derivative of {tex}\sqrt{x}{/tex} is {tex}\frac{1}{2} x^{-\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}}{/tex}.
Thus, we use the substitution {tex}\sqrt{x}=t{/tex} so that {tex}\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{x}} d x=d t{/tex} giving dx = 2t dt.
Thus, {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{4} \sqrt{x} \sec ^{2} \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{2 t \tan ^{4} t \sec ^{2} t d t}{t}{/tex} = {tex}2 \int \tan ^{4} t \sec ^{2} t d t{/tex} 
Again, we make another substitution tan t = u so that sec2 t dt = du
Therefore, {tex}2 \int \tan ^{4} t \sec ^{2} t d t=2 \int u^{4} d u=2 \frac{u^{5}}{5}+C{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2}{5} \tan ^{5} t+C{/tex} (since u = tan t)
= {tex}\frac{2}{5} \tan ^{5} \sqrt{x}+C{/tex} (since {tex}t=\sqrt{x}{/tex})
Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\tan ^{4} \sqrt{x} \sec ^{2} \sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{x}} d x=\frac{2}{5} \tan ^{5} \sqrt{x}+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Integrate the function w.r.t. x: {tex}\frac{\sin \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
solution_text: Derivative of {tex}\tan ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} . Thus, we use the substitution tan -1 x = t so that {tex}\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}=d t{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{\sin \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)}{1+x^{2}} d x=\int \sin t d t{/tex} = -cos t + C = -cos(tan -1 x) + C
solution_html:
Derivative of {tex}\tan ^{-1} x=\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}. Thus, we use the substitution tan-1 x = t so that {tex}\frac{d x}{1+x^{2}}=d t{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{\sin \left(\tan ^{-1} x\right)}{1+x^{2}} d x=\int \sin t d t{/tex} = -cos t + C = -cos(tan-1x) + C

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \sin ^{3} x \cos ^{2} x d x{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \sin ^{3} x \cos ^{2} x d x{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}\int \sin ^{3} x \cos ^{2} x d x=\int \sin ^{2} x \cos ^{2} x(\sin x) d x{/tex} = {tex}\int\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right) \cos ^{2} x(\sin x) d x{/tex} Put t = cos x so that dt = -sin x dx Therefore, {tex}\int \sin ^{2} x \cos ^{2} x(\sin x) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\int\left(1-t^{2}\right) t^{2} d t{/tex} = {tex}-\int\left(t^{2}-t^{4}\right) d t=-\left(\frac{t^{3}}{3}-\frac{t^{5}}{5}\right)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cos ^{3} x+\frac{1}{5} \cos ^{5} x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
We have
{tex}\int \sin ^{3} x \cos ^{2} x d x=\int \sin ^{2} x \cos ^{2} x(\sin x) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\int\left(1-\cos ^{2} x\right) \cos ^{2} x(\sin x) d x{/tex} 
Put t = cos x so that dt = -sin x dx
Therefore, {tex}\int \sin ^{2} x \cos ^{2} x(\sin x) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\int\left(1-t^{2}\right) t^{2} d t{/tex} 
= {tex}-\int\left(t^{2}-t^{4}\right) d t=-\left(\frac{t^{3}}{3}-\frac{t^{5}}{5}\right)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \cos ^{3} x+\frac{1}{5} \cos ^{5} x+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin (x+a)} d x{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin (x+a)} d x{/tex}
solution_text: Put x + a = t. Then dx = dt. Therefore {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin (x+a)} d x=\int \frac{\sin (t-a)}{\sin t} d t{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{\sin t \cos a-\cos t \sin a}{\sin t} d t{/tex} = {tex}\cos a \int d t-\sin a \int \cot t d t{/tex} = {tex}(\cos a) t-(\sin a)\left[\log |\sin t|+C_{1}\right]{/tex} = {tex}(\cos a)(x+a)-(\sin a)\left[\log |\sin (x+a)|+C_{1}\right]{/tex} = x cos a + a cos a - (sin a) log |sin (x + a)| - C 1 sin a Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin (x+a)} d x{/tex} = x cos a - (sin a) log |sin (x + a)| - C, where, C = -C 1 sin a + a cos a, is another arbitrary constant.
solution_html:
Put x + a = t. Then dx = dt. Therefore
{tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin (x+a)} d x=\int \frac{\sin (t-a)}{\sin t} d t{/tex} 
= {tex}\int \frac{\sin t \cos a-\cos t \sin a}{\sin t} d t{/tex} 
= {tex}\cos a \int d t-\sin a \int \cot t d t{/tex} 
= {tex}(\cos a) t-(\sin a)\left[\log |\sin t|+C_{1}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}(\cos a)(x+a)-(\sin a)\left[\log |\sin (x+a)|+C_{1}\right]{/tex} 
= x cos a + a cos a - (sin a) log |sin (x + a)| - C1 sin a
Hence, {tex}\int \frac{\sin x}{\sin (x+a)} d x{/tex} =  x cos a - (sin a) log |sin (x + a)| - C, 
where, C = -C1 sin a + a cos a, is another arbitrary constant.

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+\tan x} d x{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{1}{1+\tan x} d x{/tex}
solution_text: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{1+\tan x}=\int \frac{\cos x d x}{\cos x+\sin x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(\cos x+\sin x+\cos x-\sin x) d x}{\cos x+\sin x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{C_{1}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x{/tex} ...(i) Now, consider I = {tex}\int \frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x{/tex} Put cos x + sin x = t so that (cos x - sin x) dx = dt Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{d t}{t}=\log |t|+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\log |\cos x+\sin x|+\mathrm{C}_{2}{/tex} Putting it in (i), we get {tex}\int \frac{d x}{1+\tan x}=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \log |\cos x+\sin x|+\frac{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \log |\cos x+\sin x|+\frac{C_{1}}{2}+\frac{C_{2}}{2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \log |\cos x+\sin x|+\mathrm{C},\left(\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{2}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{1+\tan x}=\int \frac{\cos x d x}{\cos x+\sin x}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(\cos x+\sin x+\cos x-\sin x) d x}{\cos x+\sin x}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{C_{1}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x{/tex}  ...(i)
Now, consider I = {tex}\int \frac{\cos x-\sin x}{\cos x+\sin x} d x{/tex} 
Put cos x + sin x = t so that (cos x - sin x) dx = dt
Therefore, {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int \frac{d t}{t}=\log |t|+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\log |\cos x+\sin x|+\mathrm{C}_{2}{/tex}
Putting it in (i), we get
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{1+\tan x}=\frac{x}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \log |\cos x+\sin x|+\frac{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{2}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \log |\cos x+\sin x|+\frac{C_{1}}{2}+\frac{C_{2}}{2}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{1}{2} \log |\cos x+\sin x|+\mathrm{C},\left(\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{2}+\frac{\mathrm{C}_{2}}{2}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: cos 2x = 2 cos 2 x - 1 {tex}\cos ^{2} x=\frac{1+\cos 2 x}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \cos ^{2} x d x=\frac{1}{2} \int(1+\cos 2 x) d x=\frac{1}{2} \int d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \cos 2 x d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{1}{4} \sin 2 x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
cos 2x = 2 cos2 x - 1
{tex}\cos ^{2} x=\frac{1+\cos 2 x}{2}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \cos ^{2} x d x=\frac{1}{2} \int(1+\cos 2 x) d x=\frac{1}{2} \int d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \cos 2 x d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{x}{2}+\frac{1}{4} \sin 2 x+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: As we know , sin x cos y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}[\sin (x+y)+\sin (x-y)]{/tex} Then , {tex}\int \sin 2 x \cos 3 x d x=\frac{1}{2}\left[\int \sin 5 x d x-\int \sin x d x\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{1}{5} \cos 5 x+\cos x\right]+C{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{10} \cos 5 x+\frac{1}{2} \cos x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
As we know , sin x cos y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}[\sin (x+y)+\sin (x-y)]{/tex} 
Then , {tex}\int \sin 2 x \cos 3 x d x=\frac{1}{2}\left[\int \sin 5 x d x-\int \sin x d x\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[-\frac{1}{5} \cos 5 x+\cos x\right]+C{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{10} \cos 5 x+\frac{1}{2} \cos x+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: From the identity sin 3x = 3 sin x - 4 sin 3 x, we find that {tex}\sin ^{3} x=\frac{3 \sin x-\sin 3 x}{4}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \sin ^{3} x d x=\frac{3}{4} \int \sin x d x-\frac{1}{4} \int \sin 3 x d x{/tex} Integral = {tex}-\frac{3}{4} \cos x+\frac{1}{12} \cos 3 x+c{/tex}
solution_html:
From the identity sin 3x = 3 sin x - 4 sin3 x, we find that
{tex}\sin ^{3} x=\frac{3 \sin x-\sin 3 x}{4}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\int \sin ^{3} x d x=\frac{3}{4} \int \sin x d x-\frac{1}{4} \int \sin 3 x d x{/tex} 
Integral = {tex}-\frac{3}{4} \cos x+\frac{1}{12} \cos 3 x+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let I = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{2 x-x^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{1-(x-1)^{2}}}{/tex} Put x - 1 = t. Then dx = dt. Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{2 x-x^{2}}}=\int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{1-t^{2}}}=\sin ^{-1}(t)+C{/tex} = sin -1 (x-1) + C
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{2 x-x^{2}}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{1-(x-1)^{2}}}{/tex}
Put x - 1 = t. Then dx = dt.
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{2 x-x^{2}}}=\int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{1-t^{2}}}=\sin ^{-1}(t)+C{/tex} 
= sin-1 (x-1) + C

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: We have x 2 - 6x + 13 = x 2 - 6x + 3 2 - 3 2 + 13 = (x - 3) 2 + 4 So, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-6 x+13}=\int \frac{1}{(x-3)^{2}+2^{2}} d x{/tex} Let x – 3 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = dt Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-6 x+13}=\int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+2^{2}}=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{t}{2}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x-3}{2}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
We have x2 - 6x + 13 = x2 - 6x + 32 - 32 + 13 = (x - 3)2 + 4
So, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-6 x+13}=\int \frac{1}{(x-3)^{2}+2^{2}} d x{/tex} 
Let x – 3 = t {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} dx = dt
Therefore, 
{tex}\int \frac{d x}{x^{2}-6 x+13}=\int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+2^{2}}=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{t}{2}+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x-3}{2}+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{3 x^{2}+13 x-10}{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{3 x^{2}+13 x-10}{/tex}
solution_text: We write the denominator of the integrand, 3x 2 + 13x - 10 = {tex}3\left(x^{2}+\frac{13 x}{3}-\frac{10}{3}\right){/tex} = {tex}3\left[\left(x+\frac{13}{6}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{17}{6}\right)^{2}\right]{/tex} (completing the square) Thus, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{3 x^{2}+13 x-10}=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{13}{6}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{17}{6}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Put {tex}x+\frac{13}{6}=t{/tex} . Then dx = dt Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{3 x^{2}+13 x-10}=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}-\left(\frac{17}{6}\right)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{3 \times 2 \times \frac{17}{6}} \log \left|\frac{t-\frac{17}{6}}{t+\frac{17}{6}}\right|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{17} \log \left|\frac{x+\frac{13}{6}-\frac{17}{6}}{x+\frac{13}{6}+\frac{17}{6}}\right|+C_{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{17} \log \left|\frac{6 x-4}{6 x+30}\right|+C_{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{17} \log \left|\frac{3 x-2}{x+5}\right|+C_{1}+\frac{1}{17} \log \frac{1}{3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{17} \log \left|\frac{3 x-2}{x+5}\right|+C{/tex} where, {tex}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_{1}+\frac{1}{17} \log \frac{1}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
We write the denominator of the integrand,
3x2 + 13x - 10 = {tex}3\left(x^{2}+\frac{13 x}{3}-\frac{10}{3}\right){/tex}
= {tex}3\left[\left(x+\frac{13}{6}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{17}{6}\right)^{2}\right]{/tex}  (completing the square)
Thus, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{3 x^{2}+13 x-10}=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{13}{6}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{17}{6}\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
Put {tex}x+\frac{13}{6}=t{/tex}. Then dx = dt
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{3 x^{2}+13 x-10}=\frac{1}{3} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}-\left(\frac{17}{6}\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{3 \times 2 \times \frac{17}{6}} \log \left|\frac{t-\frac{17}{6}}{t+\frac{17}{6}}\right|+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{17} \log \left|\frac{x+\frac{13}{6}-\frac{17}{6}}{x+\frac{13}{6}+\frac{17}{6}}\right|+C_{1}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{17} \log \left|\frac{6 x-4}{6 x+30}\right|+C_{1}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{17} \log \left|\frac{3 x-2}{x+5}\right|+C_{1}+\frac{1}{17} \log \frac{1}{3}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{17} \log \left|\frac{3 x-2}{x+5}\right|+C{/tex} where, {tex}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_{1}+\frac{1}{17} \log \frac{1}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5 x^{2}-2 x}}{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5 x^{2}-2 x}}{/tex}
solution_text: We have {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5 x^{2}-2 x}}=\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5\left(x^{2}-\frac{2 x}{5}\right)}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{\left(x-\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} (completing the square) Put {tex}x-\frac{1}{5}=t{/tex} . Then dx = dt Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5 x^{2}-2 x}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \log |t+\sqrt{t^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}}|+C{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \log \left|x-\frac{1}{5}+\sqrt{x^{2}-\frac{2 x}{5}}\right|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
We have {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5 x^{2}-2 x}}=\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5\left(x^{2}-\frac{2 x}{5}\right)}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{\left(x-\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} (completing the square)
Put {tex}x-\frac{1}{5}=t{/tex}. Then dx = dt
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5 x^{2}-2 x}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \log |t+\sqrt{t^{2}-\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^{2}}|+C{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{\sqrt{5}} \log \left|x-\frac{1}{5}+\sqrt{x^{2}-\frac{2 x}{5}}\right|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x{/tex}
solution_text: Let, I = {tex}∫\frac{(x+2)}{(2x^2+6x+5)}dx{/tex} we express {tex}x+2=\mathrm{A} \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right)+\mathrm{B}{/tex} = A(4x + 6) + B Equating the coefficients of x and the constant terms from both sides, we get 4A = 1 and 6A + B = 2 or {tex}A=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{4 x+6}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{I}_{1}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex} .....(i) In I 1 , put 2x 2 + 6x + 5 = t, so that (4x + 6) dx = dt Therefore, {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{d t}{t}=\log |t|+C_{1}{/tex} = log |2x 2 + 6x + 5| + C .....(ii) and {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x+\frac{5}{2}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} Put {tex}x+\frac{3}{2}=t{/tex} , so that dx = dt, we get {tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \tan ^{-1} 2 t+C_{2}{/tex} {tex}\tan ^{-1} 2\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}_{2}{/tex} ......(iii) Using (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x=\frac{1}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right|+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} where, {tex}C=\frac{C_{1}}{4}+\frac{C_{2}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, I = {tex}∫\frac{(x+2)}{(2x^2+6x+5)}dx{/tex}
we express
{tex}x+2=\mathrm{A} \frac{d}{d x}\left(2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right)+\mathrm{B}{/tex} = A(4x + 6) + B
Equating the coefficients of x and the constant terms from both sides, we get
4A = 1 and 6A + B = 2 or {tex}A=\frac{1}{4}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{1}{2}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{4} \int \frac{4 x+6}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x+\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{4} \mathrm{I}_{1}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{I}_{2}{/tex}  .....(i)
In I1, put 2x2 + 6x + 5 = t, so that (4x + 6) dx = dt
Therefore, {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{d t}{t}=\log |t|+C_{1}{/tex} 
= log |2x2 + 6x +  5| + C .....(ii)
and {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d x}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+3 x+\frac{5}{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}{/tex} 
Put {tex}x+\frac{3}{2}=t{/tex}, so that dx = dt, we get
{tex}I_{2}=\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}+\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{2}}=\frac{1}{2 \times \frac{1}{2}} \tan ^{-1} 2 t+C_{2}{/tex} 
{tex}\tan ^{-1} 2\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)+\mathrm{C}_{2}=\tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}_{2}{/tex}  ......(iii)
Using (ii) and (iii) in (i), we get
{tex}\int \frac{x+2}{2 x^{2}+6 x+5} d x=\frac{1}{4} \log \left|2 x^{2}+6 x+5\right|+\frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1}(2 x+3)+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
where, {tex}C=\frac{C_{1}}{4}+\frac{C_{2}}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
question_html: Find the integral: {tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
solution_text: Given integral is: {tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} Let us express {tex}x+3=A \frac{d}{d x}\left(5-4 x-x^{2}\right)+B{/tex} = A (- 4 - 2x) + B Equating the coefficients of x and the constant terms from both sides, we get - 2A = 1 and -4 A + B = 3, i.e., {tex}A=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and B = 1 Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}} d x=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(-4-2 x) d x}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{2} I_{1}+I_{2}{/tex} ........(i) In I 1 , put 5 - 4x - x 2 = t, so that (- 4 - 2x) dx = dt. Therefore, {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{(-4-2 x) d x}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}=\int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}=2 \sqrt{t}+C_{1}{/tex} = {tex}2 \sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} .......(ii) Now consider {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}=\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{9-(x+2)^{2}}}{/tex} Put x + 2 = t, so that dx = dt. Therefore, {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{3^{2}-t^{2}}}=\sin ^{-1} \frac{t}{3}+C_{2}{/tex} = {tex}\sin ^{-1} \frac{x+2}{3}+C_{2}{/tex} .......(iii) Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we obtain {tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}=-\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{x+2}{3}+\mathrm{C}, {/tex} where {tex}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_{2}-\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Given integral is:{tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}} d x{/tex}
Let us express
{tex}x+3=A \frac{d}{d x}\left(5-4 x-x^{2}\right)+B{/tex} = A (- 4 - 2x) + B
Equating the coefficients of x and the constant terms from both sides, we get
- 2A = 1 and -4 A + B = 3, i.e., {tex}A=-\frac{1}{2}{/tex} and B = 1
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}} d x=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(-4-2 x) d x}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}+\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{2} I_{1}+I_{2}{/tex}  ........(i)
In I1, put 5 - 4x - x2 = t, so that (- 4 - 2x) dx = dt.
Therefore, {tex}I_{1}=\int \frac{(-4-2 x) d x}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}=\int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}=2 \sqrt{t}+C_{1}{/tex} 
= {tex}2 \sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}+\mathrm{C}_{1}{/tex} .......(ii)
Now consider {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}=\int \frac{d x}{\sqrt{9-(x+2)^{2}}}{/tex} 
Put x + 2 = t, so that dx = dt.
Therefore, {tex}I_{2}=\int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{3^{2}-t^{2}}}=\sin ^{-1} \frac{t}{3}+C_{2}{/tex} 
= {tex}\sin ^{-1} \frac{x+2}{3}+C_{2}{/tex} .......(iii)
Substituting (ii) and (iii) in (i), we obtain
{tex}\int \frac{x+3}{\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}}=-\sqrt{5-4 x-x^{2}}+\sin ^{-1} \frac{x+2}{3}+\mathrm{C}, {/tex} where {tex}\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_{2}-\frac{\mathrm{C}_{1}}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: The integral is a proper rational function. Therefore, by using the form of partial fraction, we write {tex}\frac{1}{(x+1)(x+2)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{x+1}+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{x+2}{/tex} ......(i) where, real numbers A and B are to be determined suitably. This gives 1 = A (x + 2) + B (x + 1). Equating the coefficients of x and the constant term, we get A + B = 0 and 2A + B = 1 Solving these equations, we get A = 1 and B = -1. Thus, the integral is given by {tex}\frac{1}{(x+1)(x+2)}=\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{-1}{x+2}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(x+1)(x+2)}=\int \frac{d x}{x+1}-\int \frac{d x}{x+2}{/tex} = log |x + 1| - log |x + 2| + C = {tex}\log \left|\frac{x+1}{x+2}\right|+C{/tex}
solution_html:
The integral is a proper rational function.
Therefore, by using the form of partial fraction, we write
{tex}\frac{1}{(x+1)(x+2)}=\frac{\mathrm{A}}{x+1}+\frac{\mathrm{B}}{x+2}{/tex}  ......(i)
where, real numbers A and B are to be determined suitably. This gives
1 = A (x + 2) + B (x + 1).
Equating the coefficients of x and the constant term, we get
A + B = 0 and 2A + B = 1
Solving these equations, we get A = 1 and B = -1.
Thus, the integral is given by
{tex}\frac{1}{(x+1)(x+2)}=\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{-1}{x+2}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{d x}{(x+1)(x+2)}=\int \frac{d x}{x+1}-\int \frac{d x}{x+2}{/tex} 
= log |x + 1| -  log |x + 2| + C
= {tex}\log \left|\frac{x+1}{x+2}\right|+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Here the integrand {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-5 x+6}{/tex} is not proper rational function, so we divide x 2 + 1 by x 2 - 5x + 6 and find that {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-5 x+6}=1+\frac{5 x-5}{x^{2}-5 x+6}=1+\frac{5 x-5}{(x-2)(x-3)}{/tex} Let {tex}\frac{5 x-5}{(x-2)(x-3)}=\frac{A}{x-2}+\frac{B}{x-3}{/tex} So that 5x - 5 = A (x - 3) + B (x - 2) Equating the coefficients of x and constant terms on both sides, we get A + B = 5 and 3A + 2B = 5. Solving these equations, we get A = -5 and B = 10 Thus, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-5 x+6}=1-\frac{5}{x-2}+\frac{10}{x-3}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-5 x+6} d x=\int d x-5 \int \frac{1}{x-2} d x+10 \int \frac{d x}{x-3}{/tex} = x - 5 log | x - 2| + 10 log | x - 3| + C.
solution_html:
Here the integrand {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-5 x+6}{/tex} is not proper rational function, so we divide x2 + 1 by x2 - 5x + 6 and find that
{tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-5 x+6}=1+\frac{5 x-5}{x^{2}-5 x+6}=1+\frac{5 x-5}{(x-2)(x-3)}{/tex} 
Let {tex}\frac{5 x-5}{(x-2)(x-3)}=\frac{A}{x-2}+\frac{B}{x-3}{/tex} 
So that 5x - 5 = A (x - 3) + B (x - 2)
Equating the coefficients of x and constant terms on both sides,
we get A + B = 5 and 3A + 2B = 5.
Solving these equations, we get A = -5 and B = 10
Thus, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-5 x+6}=1-\frac{5}{x-2}+\frac{10}{x-3}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x^{2}+1}{x^{2}-5 x+6} d x=\int d x-5 \int \frac{1}{x-2} d x+10 \int \frac{d x}{x-3}{/tex} 
= x - 5 log | x - 2| + 10 log | x - 3| + C.

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: We write {tex}\frac{3 x-2}{(x+1)^{2}(x+3)}=\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{(x+1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{x+3}{/tex} So that 3x - 2 = A (x + 1) (x + 3) + B (x + 3) + C (x + 1) 2 = A (x 2 + 4x + 3) + B (x + 3) + C (x 2 + 2x + 1) Comparing coefficient of x 2 , x and constant term on both sides, we get A + C = 0, 4A + B + 2C = 3 and 3A + 3B + C = -2. Solving these equations, we get {tex}A=\frac{11}{4}, B=\frac{-5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}C=\frac{-11}{4}{/tex} . Thus the integrand is given by {tex}\frac{3 x-2}{(x+1)^{2}(x+3)}=\frac{11}{4(x+1)}-\frac{5}{2(x+1)^{2}}-\frac{11}{4(x+3)}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{3 x-2}{(x+1)^{2}(x+3)}=\frac{11}{4} \int \frac{d x}{x+1}-\frac{5}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x+1)^{2}}-\frac{11}{4} \int \frac{d x}{x+3}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{11}{4} \log |x+1|+\frac{5}{2(x+1)}-\frac{11}{4} \log |x+3|+\mathrm{C}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{11}{4} \log \left|\frac{x+1}{x+3}\right|+\frac{5}{2(x+1)}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
We write 
{tex}\frac{3 x-2}{(x+1)^{2}(x+3)}=\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{(x+1)^{2}}+\frac{C}{x+3}{/tex} 
So that 3x - 2 = A (x + 1) (x + 3) + B (x + 3) + C (x + 1)2 
= A (x2 + 4x + 3) + B (x + 3) + C (x2 + 2x + 1)
Comparing coefficient of x2, x and constant term on both sides,
we get A + C = 0, 4A + B + 2C = 3 and 3A + 3B + C = -2. Solving these equations, we get
{tex}A=\frac{11}{4}, B=\frac{-5}{2}{/tex} and {tex}C=\frac{-11}{4}{/tex}. Thus the integrand is given by
{tex}\frac{3 x-2}{(x+1)^{2}(x+3)}=\frac{11}{4(x+1)}-\frac{5}{2(x+1)^{2}}-\frac{11}{4(x+3)}{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{3 x-2}{(x+1)^{2}(x+3)}=\frac{11}{4} \int \frac{d x}{x+1}-\frac{5}{2} \int \frac{d x}{(x+1)^{2}}-\frac{11}{4} \int \frac{d x}{x+3}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{11}{4} \log |x+1|+\frac{5}{2(x+1)}-\frac{11}{4} \log |x+3|+\mathrm{C}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{11}{4} \log \left|\frac{x+1}{x+3}\right|+\frac{5}{2(x+1)}+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Consider {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} and put x 2 = y Then, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}=\frac{y}{(y+1)(y+4)}{/tex} Write, {tex}\frac{y}{(y+1)(y+4)}=\frac{A}{y+1}+\frac{B}{y+4}{/tex} So that, y = A (y + 4) + B (y + 1) Comparing coefficients of y and constant terms on both sides, we get A + B = 1 and 4A + B = 0, which give {tex}A=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} Thus, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}=-\frac{1}{3\left(x^{2}+1\right)}+\frac{4}{3\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1} x+\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}+C{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1} x+\frac{2}{3} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Consider {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} and put x2 = y
Then, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}=\frac{y}{(y+1)(y+4)}{/tex} 
Write, {tex}\frac{y}{(y+1)(y+4)}=\frac{A}{y+1}+\frac{B}{y+4}{/tex} 
So that, y = A (y + 4) + B (y + 1)
Comparing coefficients of y and constant terms on both sides,
we get A + B = 1 and 4A + B = 0, which give
{tex}A=-\frac{1}{3}{/tex} and {tex}B=\frac{4}{3}{/tex} 
Thus, {tex}\frac{x^{2}}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)\left(x^{2}+4\right)}=-\frac{1}{3\left(x^{2}+1\right)}+\frac{4}{3\left(x^{2}+4\right)}{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1} x+\frac{4}{3} \times \frac{1}{2} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}+C{/tex} 
= {tex}-\frac{1}{3} \tan ^{-1} x+\frac{2}{3} \tan ^{-1} \frac{x}{2}+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let {tex}y=\sin \phi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow d y=\cos \phi d \phi{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{(3 \sin \phi-2) \cos \phi}{5-\cos ^{2} \phi-4 \sin \phi} d \phi{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{(3 y-2) d y}{5-\left(1-y^{2}\right)-4 y}{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{3 y-2}{y^{2}-4 y+4} d y{/tex} Now, we write {tex}\frac{3 y-2}{(y-2)^{2}}=\frac{A}{y-2}+\frac{B}{(y-2)^{2}}{/tex} Therefore, 3y - 2 = A (y - 2) + B Comparing the coefficients of y and constant term, we get A = 3 and B = 4. for y = 2, y= 0 Therefore, the required integral is given by {tex}I=\int\left[\frac{3}{y-2}+\frac{4}{(y-2)^{2}}\right] d y{/tex} = {tex}3 \int \frac{d y}{y-2}+4 \int \frac{d y}{(y-2)^{2}}{/tex} = {tex}3 \log |y-2|+4\left(-\frac{1}{y-2}\right)+C{/tex} = {tex}3 \log |\sin \phi-2|+\frac{4}{2-\sin \phi}+C{/tex} = {tex}3 \log (2-\sin \phi)+\frac{4}{2-\sin \phi}+C{/tex} (since, sin {tex}\phi {/tex} {tex}\in{/tex} [-1,1], sin {tex}\phi {/tex} < 2, 2 - sin {tex}\phi {/tex} is always positive)
solution_html:
Let {tex}y=\sin \phi{/tex}  {tex}\Rightarrow d y=\cos \phi d \phi{/tex} 
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{(3 \sin \phi-2) \cos \phi}{5-\cos ^{2} \phi-4 \sin \phi} d \phi{/tex} = {tex}\int \frac{(3 y-2) d y}{5-\left(1-y^{2}\right)-4 y}{/tex} 
= {tex}\int \frac{3 y-2}{y^{2}-4 y+4} d y{/tex} 
Now, we write {tex}\frac{3 y-2}{(y-2)^{2}}=\frac{A}{y-2}+\frac{B}{(y-2)^{2}}{/tex} 
Therefore, 3y - 2 = A (y - 2) + B
Comparing the coefficients of y and constant term,
we get A = 3 and B = 4. for y = 2, y= 0
Therefore, the required integral is given by
{tex}I=\int\left[\frac{3}{y-2}+\frac{4}{(y-2)^{2}}\right] d y{/tex} = {tex}3 \int \frac{d y}{y-2}+4 \int \frac{d y}{(y-2)^{2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}3 \log |y-2|+4\left(-\frac{1}{y-2}\right)+C{/tex} 
= {tex}3 \log |\sin \phi-2|+\frac{4}{2-\sin \phi}+C{/tex} 
= {tex}3 \log (2-\sin \phi)+\frac{4}{2-\sin \phi}+C{/tex} (since, sin {tex}\phi {/tex} {tex}\in{/tex}[-1,1], sin {tex}\phi {/tex}< 2, 2 - sin {tex}\phi {/tex} is always positive)

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let, {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} By partial fractions {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} x 2 + x + 1 = A(x 2 + 1) + Bx + C(x + 2) =Ax 2 + A + Bx 2 + 2Bx + Cx + 2Cx 2 + x + 1 = x 2 (A + B) + x(2B + C) + A + 2C On comparing coefficients of equation (i), we get 1 = A + B and 1 = 2B + C On solving above three equations, we get {tex}A=\frac{3}{5}{/tex} , {tex} B=\frac{2}{5} {/tex} and {tex}C=\frac{1}{5}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5(x+2)}+\frac{\frac{2}{5} x+\frac{1}{5}}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right) d x}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x+\frac{2}{5} \int \frac{x}{x^{2}+1} d x +\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|+\frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let, {tex}I=\int \frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
By partial fractions
{tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{A}{x+2}+\frac{B x+C}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
x2 + x + 1 = A(x2 + 1) + Bx + C(x + 2)
=Ax2 + A + Bx2 + 2Bx + Cx + 2Cx2 + x + 1
= x2(A + B) + x(2B + C) + A + 2C
On comparing coefficients of equation (i), we get
1 = A + B and 1 = 2B + C
On solving above three equations, we get
{tex}A=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}, {tex} B=\frac{2}{5} {/tex} and {tex}C=\frac{1}{5}{/tex}
Hence, {tex}\frac{x^{2}+x+1}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5(x+2)}+\frac{\frac{2}{5} x+\frac{1}{5}}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{\left(x^{2}+x+1\right) d x}{(x+2)\left(x^{2}+1\right)}=\frac{3}{5} \int \frac{1}{x+2} d x+\frac{2}{5} \int \frac{x}{x^{2}+1} d x +\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{1}{\left(x^{2}+1\right)} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{2 x}{x^{2}+1}+\frac{1}{5} \int \frac{d x}{x^{2}+1}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{3}{5} \log |x+2|+\frac{1}{5} \log \left|x^{2}+1\right|+\frac{1}{5} \tan ^{-1} x+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Put f (x) = x (first function) and g (x) = cos x (second function). Then, integration by parts gives {tex}\int x \cos x d x=x \int \cos x d x-\int\left[\frac{d}{d x}(x) \int \cos x d x\right] d x{/tex} = {tex}x \sin x-\int \sin x d x=x \sin x+\cos x+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
Put f (x) = x (first function) and g (x) = cos x (second function).
Then, integration by parts gives
{tex}\int x \cos x d x=x \int \cos x d x-\int\left[\frac{d}{d x}(x) \int \cos x d x\right] d x{/tex} 
= {tex}x \sin x-\int \sin x d x=x \sin x+\cos x+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: We take log x as the first function and the constant function 1 as the second function. Hence, {tex}\int(\log x ).1 d x=\log x \int 1 d x-\int\left[\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \int 1 d x\right] d x{/tex} = {tex}(\log x) \cdot x-\int \frac{1}{x} x d x=x \log x-x+C{/tex}
solution_html:
We take log x as the first function and the constant function 1 as the second function. 
Hence, {tex}\int(\log x ).1 d x=\log x \int 1 d x-\int\left[\frac{d}{d x}(\log x) \int 1 d x\right] d x{/tex} 
= {tex}(\log x) \cdot x-\int \frac{1}{x} x d x=x \log x-x+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Take first function as x and second function as e x . The integral of the second function is e x Therefore, {tex}\int x e^{x} d x=x e^{x}-\int 1 \cdot e^{x} d x=x e^{x}-e^{x}+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Take first function as x and second function as ex. The integral of the second function is ex 
Therefore, {tex}\int x e^{x} d x=x e^{x}-\int 1 \cdot e^{x} d x=x e^{x}-e^{x}+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let first function be sin -1 x and second function be {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} . First we find the integral of the second function, i.e., {tex}\int \frac{x d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} Put t =1 - x 2 . Then dt = -2x dx Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}=-\sqrt{t}=-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int \frac{x \sin ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} = {tex}(\sin ^{-1} x){/tex} {tex}\int \frac{x d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}-\int [\frac d{dx} sin^{-1}x.\int \frac{x d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} ] d x{/tex} = {tex}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)(-\sqrt{1-x^{2}})-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}) d x{/tex} = {tex}-\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \sin ^{-1} x+x+C{/tex} = {tex}x-\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \sin ^{-1} x+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let first function be sin-1 x and second function be {tex}\frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex}.
First we find the integral of the second function, i.e., {tex}\int \frac{x d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} 
Put t =1 - x2. Then dt = -2x dx
Therefore, {tex}\int \frac{x d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}=-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d t}{\sqrt{t}}=-\sqrt{t}=-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}{/tex} 
Hence, {tex}\int \frac{x \sin ^{-1} x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} d x{/tex} = {tex}(\sin ^{-1} x){/tex}{tex}\int \frac{x d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}-\int [\frac d{dx} sin^{-1}x.\int \frac{x d x}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}} ] d x{/tex}
= {tex}\left(\sin ^{-1} x\right)(-\sqrt{1-x^{2}})-\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-x^{2}}}(-\sqrt{1-x^{2}}) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}-\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \sin ^{-1} x+x+C{/tex} 
= {tex}x-\sqrt{1-x^{2}} \sin ^{-1} x+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Take e x as the first function and sin x as second function. Using Integrating by parts, we have {tex}\mathrm{I}=\int e^{x} \sin x d x=e^{x}(-\cos x)+\int e^{x} \cos x d x{/tex} = -e x cos x + {tex}I_1{/tex} ......(i) Taking e x and cos x as the first and second functions, respectively, in {tex}I_1{/tex} , we get {tex}I_{1}=e^{x} \sin x-\int e^{x} \sin x d x{/tex} Substituting the value of {tex}I_1{/tex} in (i), we get {tex}I{/tex} = -e x cos x + e x sin x - {tex}I{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow~2I{/tex} = e x (sin x - cos x) Hence, {tex}I=\int e^{x} \sin x d x=\frac{e^{x}}{2}(\sin x-\cos x)+C{/tex}
solution_html:
Take ex as the first function and sin x as second function.
Using Integrating by parts, we have
{tex}\mathrm{I}=\int e^{x} \sin x d x=e^{x}(-\cos x)+\int e^{x} \cos x d x{/tex} 
= -ex cos x + {tex}I_1{/tex} ......(i)
Taking ex and cos x as the first and second functions, respectively, in {tex}I_1{/tex}, we get
{tex}I_{1}=e^{x} \sin x-\int e^{x} \sin x d x{/tex} 
Substituting the value of {tex}I_1{/tex} in (i), we get
{tex}I{/tex} = -ex cos x + ex sin x - {tex}I{/tex}  
{tex}\Rightarrow~2I{/tex} = ex (sin x - cos x)
Hence, {tex}I=\int e^{x} \sin x d x=\frac{e^{x}}{2}(\sin x-\cos x)+C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: We have {tex}I=\int e^{x}\left(\tan ^{-1} x+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} Consider f(x) = tan -1 x, then f′(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex} Thus, the given integrand is of the form e x [f(x) + f′(x)]. Therefore, {tex}I=\int e^{x}\left(\tan ^{-1} x+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} = e x tan -1 x + C
solution_html:
We have {tex}I=\int e^{x}\left(\tan ^{-1} x+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} 
Consider f(x) = tan-1 x, then f′(x) = {tex}\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}{/tex}
Thus, the given integrand is of the form ex [f(x) + f′(x)].
Therefore, {tex}I=\int e^{x}\left(\tan ^{-1} x+\frac{1}{1+x^{2}}\right) d x{/tex} = ex tan-1 x + C

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Find {tex}\int \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) e ^ { x } } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x.{/tex}
question_html: Find {tex}\int \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) e ^ { x } } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x.{/tex}
solution_text: According to the question, {tex}I = \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 + 2 x - 2 x \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } - 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x [\therefore (a+b)^2 = a^2 +b^2+2ab]{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( 1 - \frac { 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int e ^ { x } d x - 2 \int e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x+1 } { ( x + 1 )^2 } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex} Consider {tex}f ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { x + 1 },{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex} Thus, the above integral is of the form {tex}\because \int e ^ { x } f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C {/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad I = e ^ { x } - 2 e ^ { x }\Big[ \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } \Big]+ C{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 2 } { x + 1 } \right) + C {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex} {tex}\therefore \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}I = \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 + 2 x - 2 x \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } - 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x [\therefore (a+b)^2 = a^2 +b^2+2ab]{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } \left( 1 - \frac { 2 x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int e ^ { x } d x - 2 \int e ^ { x } \cdot \frac { x } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 1 } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}

{tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x+1 } { ( x + 1 )^2 } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= e ^ { x } - 2 \int e ^ { x } \left( \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } + \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } \right) d x{/tex}
Consider {tex}f ( x ) = \frac { 1 } { x + 1 },{/tex}{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}f ^ { \prime } ( x ) = \frac { ( - 1 ) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
Thus, the above integral is of the form
{tex}\because \int e ^ { x } f ( x ) + f ^ { \prime } ( x ) d x = e ^ { x } f ( x ) + C {/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad I = e ^ { x } - 2 e ^ { x }\Big[ \frac { 1 } { ( x + 1 ) } \Big]+ C{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x + 1 - 2 } { x + 1 } \right) + C {/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \int e ^ { x } \frac { \left( x ^ { 2 } + 1 \right) } { ( x + 1 ) ^ { 2 } } d x = e ^ { x } \left( \frac { x - 1 } { x + 1 } \right) + C{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Note that {tex}\int \sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+5} d x=\int \sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+4} d x{/tex} Put x + 1 = y, so that dx = dy. Then {tex}\int \sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+5} d x=\int \sqrt{y^{2}+2^{2}} d y{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2} y \sqrt{y^{2}+4}+\frac{4}{2} \log |y+\sqrt{y^{2}+4}|+C{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+5}+2 \log |x+1+\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+5}|+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
solution_html:
Note that
{tex}\int \sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+5} d x=\int \sqrt{(x+1)^{2}+4} d x{/tex} 
Put x + 1 = y, so that dx = dy. Then
{tex}\int \sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+5} d x=\int \sqrt{y^{2}+2^{2}} d y{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2} y \sqrt{y^{2}+4}+\frac{4}{2} \log |y+\sqrt{y^{2}+4}|+C{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+5}+2 \log |x+1+\sqrt{x^{2}+2 x+5}|+\mathrm{C}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let I = {tex}\int \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}} \;d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+2 x-3\right)} d x{/tex} {tex}=\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+1+2 x-3-1\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left[(x+1)^{2}-4\right]} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because\int\sqrt{a^2-x^2}dx=\frac x2\sqrt{a^2-x^2}+\frac{a^2}2\sin^{-1}\left(\frac xa\right)+c\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} +\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+1}{2}\right)+c{/tex} {tex}\therefore \ \mathrm{I}=\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}=\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{(x+1)}{2}+c{/tex}
solution_html:
Let I = {tex}\int \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}} \;d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+2 x-3\right)} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\int \sqrt{-\left(x^{2}+1+2 x-3-1\right)} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{-\left[(x+1)^{2}-4\right]} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex}
= {tex}\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex} {tex}\left[\because\int\sqrt{a^2-x^2}dx=\frac x2\sqrt{a^2-x^2}+\frac{a^2}2\sin^{-1}\left(\frac xa\right)+c\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{4-(x+1)^{2}} +\frac{4}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{x+1}{2}\right)+c{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \ \mathrm{I}=\int \sqrt{2^{2}-(x+1)^{2}} d x{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{1}{2}(x+1) \sqrt{3-2 x-x^{2}}+2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{(x+1)}{2}+c{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: f(x) is continuous in [2, 3] {tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } h\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{n - 1} f (a + rh){/tex} where {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{n}}{/tex} Here, {tex}\int_{2}^{3}\left(x^{2}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{1}{n}} \right)\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{n - 1} f \left( {2 + \left( {\frac{r}{n}} \right)} \right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{1}{n}} \right)\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{n - 1} {{{\left( {2 + \left( {\frac{r}{n}} \right)} \right)}^2}}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{1}{n}} \right)\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{n - 1} {\left( {\frac{{{r^2}}}{{{n^2}}} + 4 + \frac{{4r}}{n}} \right)}{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left( {\frac{{(n - 1)(n)(2n - 1)}}{{6{n^2}}} + 4n + \frac{{4(n - 1)(n)}}{{2n}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left( {\frac{{\left( {{n^2} - n} \right)(2n - 1)}}{{6{n^2}}} + 4n + \frac{{2\left( {{n^2} - n} \right)}}{n}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{\left(2 n^{3}-2 n^{2}-n^{2}+n\right)}{6 n^{2}}+4 n+\frac{2\left(n^{2}-n\right)}{n}\right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left( {\frac{{\left( {2{n^3} - 3{n^2} + n} \right) + \left( {24{n^3}} \right) + \left( {12{n^3} - 12{n^2}} \right)}}{{6{n^2}}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left( {\frac{{38{n^3} - 15{n^2} + n}}{{6{n^2}}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{{38{n^3} - 15{n^2} + n}}{{6{n^3}}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{{38}}{6}} \right) - \left( {\frac{{15}}{{6n}}} \right) + \left( {\frac{1}{{6{n^2}}}} \right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{38}{6}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{19}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
f(x) is continuous in [2, 3]
{tex}\int_{a}^{b} f(x) d x{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } h\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{n - 1} f (a + rh){/tex} where {tex}\mathrm{h}=\frac{\mathrm{b}-\mathrm{a}}{\mathrm{n}}{/tex} 
Here,
{tex}\int_{2}^{3}\left(x^{2}\right) d x{/tex} = {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{1}{n}} \right)\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{n - 1} f \left( {2 + \left( {\frac{r}{n}} \right)} \right){/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{1}{n}} \right)\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{n - 1} {{{\left( {2 + \left( {\frac{r}{n}} \right)} \right)}^2}}{/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{1}{n}} \right)\sum\limits_{r = 0}^{n - 1} {\left( {\frac{{{r^2}}}{{{n^2}}} + 4 + \frac{{4r}}{n}} \right)}{/tex}
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left( {\frac{{(n - 1)(n)(2n - 1)}}{{6{n^2}}} + 4n + \frac{{4(n - 1)(n)}}{{2n}}} \right){/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left( {\frac{{\left( {{n^2} - n} \right)(2n - 1)}}{{6{n^2}}} + 4n + \frac{{2\left( {{n^2} - n} \right)}}{n}} \right){/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{\left(2 n^{3}-2 n^{2}-n^{2}+n\right)}{6 n^{2}}+4 n+\frac{2\left(n^{2}-n\right)}{n}\right){/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left( {\frac{{\left( {2{n^3} - 3{n^2} + n} \right) + \left( {24{n^3}} \right) + \left( {12{n^3} - 12{n^2}} \right)}}{{6{n^2}}}} \right){/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \frac{1}{n}\left( {\frac{{38{n^3} - 15{n^2} + n}}{{6{n^2}}}} \right){/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{{38{n^3} - 15{n^2} + n}}{{6{n^3}}}} \right){/tex} 
= {tex}\mathop {\lim }\limits_{n \to \infty } \left( {\frac{{38}}{6}} \right) - \left( {\frac{{15}}{{6n}}} \right) + \left( {\frac{1}{{6{n^2}}}} \right){/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{38}{6}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{19}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let {tex}I=\int_{4}^{9} \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\left(30-x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)^{2}} d x{/tex} . We first find the antiderivative of the integrand. Put {tex}30-x^{\frac{3}{2}}=t{/tex} . Then {tex}-\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} d x=d t{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{x} d x=-\frac{2}{3} d t{/tex} Thus, {tex}\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\left(30-x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{t}\right]=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{\left(30-x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)}\right]=\mathrm{F}(x){/tex} Therefore, by the second fundamental theorem of calculus, we have I = F(9) - F(4) = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{(30-27)}-\frac{1}{30-8}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{22}\right]=\frac{19}{99}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int_{4}^{9} \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\left(30-x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)^{2}} d x{/tex}. 
We first find the antiderivative of the integrand. 
Put {tex}30-x^{\frac{3}{2}}=t{/tex}. Then {tex}-\frac{3}{2} \sqrt{x} d x=d t{/tex} or {tex}\sqrt{x} d x=-\frac{2}{3} d t{/tex}
Thus, {tex}\int \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\left(30-x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)^{2}} d x{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{2}{3} \int \frac{d t}{t^{2}}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{t}\right]=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{\left(30-x^{\frac{3}{2}}\right)}\right]=\mathrm{F}(x){/tex} 
Therefore, by the second fundamental theorem of calculus, we have
I = F(9) - F(4)
= {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{(30-27)}-\frac{1}{30-8}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}=\frac{2}{3}\left[\frac{1}{3}-\frac{1}{22}\right]=\frac{19}{99}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let {tex}I=\int_{1}^{2} \frac{x d x}{(x+1)(x+2)}{/tex} Using partial fraction, we get {tex}\frac{x}{(x+1)(x+2)}=\frac{-1}{x+1}+\frac{2}{x+2}{/tex} So, {tex}\int \frac{x d x}{(x+1)(x+2)}{/tex} = - log |x + 1| + 2log |x + 2| = F(x) Therefore, by the second fundamental theorem of calculus, we have I = F(2) - F(1) = [- log 3 + 2 log 4] - [- log 2 + 2 log 3] = -3 log 3 + log 2 + 2 log 4 = log {tex} \left(\frac{32}{27}\right){/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int_{1}^{2} \frac{x d x}{(x+1)(x+2)}{/tex} 
Using partial fraction, we get {tex}\frac{x}{(x+1)(x+2)}=\frac{-1}{x+1}+\frac{2}{x+2}{/tex} 
So, {tex}\int \frac{x d x}{(x+1)(x+2)}{/tex} = - log |x + 1| +  2log |x + 2| = F(x)
Therefore, by the second fundamental theorem of calculus, we have
I = F(2) - F(1) = [- log 3 + 2 log 4] - [- log 2 + 2 log 3]
= -3 log 3 + log 2 + 2 log 4 = log{tex} \left(\frac{32}{27}\right){/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \sin ^{3} 2 t \cos 2 t d t{/tex} . Consider {tex}\int \sin ^{3} 2 t \cos 2 t d t{/tex} Put sin 2t = u so that 2 cos 2t dt = du or cos 2t dt = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} du So {tex}\int \sin ^{3} 2 t \cos 2 t d t=\frac{1}{2} \int u^{3} d u{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{8}\left[u^{4}\right]=\frac{1}{8} \sin ^{4} 2 t=\mathrm{F}(t){/tex} Therefore, by the second fundamental theorem of integral calculus {tex}I=F\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-F(0)=\frac{1}{8}\left[\sin ^{4} \frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{4} 0\right]=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{4}} \sin ^{3} 2 t \cos 2 t d t{/tex}.
Consider {tex}\int \sin ^{3} 2 t \cos 2 t d t{/tex} 
Put sin 2t = u so that 2 cos 2t dt = du or cos 2t dt = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}du
So {tex}\int \sin ^{3} 2 t \cos 2 t d t=\frac{1}{2} \int u^{3} d u{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{1}{8}\left[u^{4}\right]=\frac{1}{8} \sin ^{4} 2 t=\mathrm{F}(t){/tex} 
Therefore, by the second fundamental theorem of integral calculus
{tex}I=F\left(\frac{\pi}{4}\right)-F(0)=\frac{1}{8}\left[\sin ^{4} \frac{\pi}{2}-\sin ^{4} 0\right]=\frac{1}{8}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Put t = x 5 + 1, then dt = 5x 4 dx. Therefore, {tex}\int 5 x^{4} \sqrt{x^{5}+1} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{t} d t=\frac{2}{3} t^{\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{2}{3}\left(x^{5}+1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int_{-1}^{1} 5 x^{4} \sqrt{x^{5}+1} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\left(x^{5}+1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_{-1}^{1}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\left(1^{5}+1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}-\left((-1)^{5}+1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[2^{\frac{3}{2}}-0^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]=\frac{2}{3}(2 \sqrt{2})=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex}
solution_html:
Put t = x5 + 1, then dt = 5x4 dx.
Therefore, {tex}\int 5 x^{4} \sqrt{x^{5}+1} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int \sqrt{t} d t=\frac{2}{3} t^{\frac{3}{2}}=\frac{2}{3}\left(x^{5}+1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}{/tex} 
Hence, {tex}\int_{-1}^{1} 5 x^{4} \sqrt{x^{5}+1} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\left(x^{5}+1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]_{-1}^{1}{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[\left(1^{5}+1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}-\left((-1)^{5}+1\right)^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2}{3}\left[2^{\frac{3}{2}}-0^{\frac{3}{2}}\right]=\frac{2}{3}(2 \sqrt{2})=\frac{4 \sqrt{2}}{3}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex} Put {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } x = t \quad \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = d t{/tex} Lower limit when x = 0, then t = 0 Upper limit when x = 1, then t = {tex}\pi / 4.{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } t d t = \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) ^ { 2 } - ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } \right] = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 32 }{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \frac { \tan ^ { - 1 } x } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x{/tex}
Put {tex}\tan ^ { - 1 } x = t \quad \Rightarrow \frac { 1 } { 1 + x ^ { 2 } } d x = d t{/tex}
Lower limit when x = 0, then t = 0
Upper limit when x = 1, then t = {tex}\pi / 4.{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } t d t = \left[ \frac { t ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { \pi / 4 } = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \left( \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) ^ { 2 } - ( 0 ) ^ { 2 } \right] = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 32 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { - 1 } ^ { 2 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x{/tex} We can observe that, {tex}\left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| = \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } - 1 < x < 0 } \\ { - \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } 0 \leq x < 1 } \\ { \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } 1 \leq x < 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex} By Splitting the intervals , we get {tex}I =\int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x +\int _ { 1} ^ { 2 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } - \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= \left[ 0 - \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \right] - \left[ \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) - 0 \right]{/tex} {tex}+ \left[ \left( \frac { 16 } { 4 } - \frac { 4 } { 2 } \right) - \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + 4 - 2 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex} {tex}= - \frac { 3 } { 4 } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } + 2{/tex} {tex} = \frac { - 3 + 6 + 8 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} = \frac { 11 } { 4 }{/tex} {tex}\therefore I = \frac { 11 } { 4 }{/tex} sq units.
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { - 1 } ^ { 2 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x{/tex}
We can observe that,
{tex}\left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| = \left\{ \begin{array} { c } { \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } - 1 < x < 0 } \\ { - \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } 0 \leq x < 1 } \\ { \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) , \text { when } 1 \leq x < 2 } \end{array} \right.{/tex}
By Splitting the intervals , we get  
{tex}I =\int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x +\int _ { 1} ^ { 2 } \left| x ^ { 3 } - x \right| d x{/tex}
 {tex}\therefore I = \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } - \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x - \int _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x + \int _ { 1 } ^ { 2 } \left( x ^ { 3 } - x \right) d x{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 0 } - \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 0 } ^ { 1 } + \left[ \frac { x ^ { 4 } } { 4 } - \frac { x ^ { 2 } } { 2 } \right] _ { 1 } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ 0 - \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \right] - \left[ \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) - 0 \right]{/tex}{tex}+ \left[ \left( \frac { 16 } { 4 } - \frac { 4 } { 2 } \right) - \left( \frac { 1 } { 4 } - \frac { 1 } { 2 } \right) \right]{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 } + 4 - 2 - \frac { 1 } { 4 } + \frac { 1 } { 2 }{/tex}
{tex}= - \frac { 3 } { 4 } + \frac { 3 } { 2 } + 2{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { - 3 + 6 + 8 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} = \frac { 11 } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex}\therefore I = \frac { 11 } { 4 }{/tex} sq units.

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let f(x) = sin 2 x f(-x) = sin 2 (-x) = sin 2 x = f(x) {tex}\therefore{/tex} function is even {tex}\therefore \int_{ - \pi /4}^{\pi /4} {{{\sin }^2}xdx = 2\int_0^{\pi /4} {{{\sin }^2}xdx} }{/tex} {tex}= \int_\limits0^{ {tex}\pi{/tex} /4} {2\left( {\frac{{1 - \cos 2x}}{2}} \right)dx} {/tex} {tex}= \int_\limits0^{ {tex}\pi{/tex} /4} {\left( {{{1 - \cos 2x}}{}} \right)dx} {/tex} {tex}= \left[ {x - \frac{{\sin 2x}}{2}} \right]_0^{\pi /4}{/tex} {tex} = \frac{\pi }{4} - \frac{1}{2}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x) = sin2x
f(-x) = sin2(-x) = sin2x = f(x)
{tex}\therefore{/tex} function is even
{tex}\therefore \int_{ - \pi /4}^{\pi /4} {{{\sin }^2}xdx = 2\int_0^{\pi /4} {{{\sin }^2}xdx} }{/tex}
{tex}= \int_\limits0^{{tex}\pi{/tex}/4} {2\left( {\frac{{1 - \cos 2x}}{2}} \right)dx} {/tex}
{tex}= \int_\limits0^{{tex}\pi{/tex}/4} {\left( {{{1 - \cos 2x}}{}} \right)dx} {/tex}
{tex}= \left[ {x - \frac{{\sin 2x}}{2}} \right]_0^{\pi /4}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{\pi }{4} - \frac{1}{2}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .
question_html: Evaluate {tex}\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}.
solution_text: According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .......(i) {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin ( \pi - x ) } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int _0^a f(x)= \int_0^a f(a-x)]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .....(ii) On Adding eqs.(i) and (ii),we get {tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi \sin x } { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x \right) } d x{/tex} Put , {tex}cos x = t{/tex} {tex}-sin x dx = dt{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow sinx dx = -dt{/tex} Lower limit , when x = 0, then t = 1 Upper limit ,when {tex}x= \pi{/tex} , then t = -1 {tex}= - \pi \int _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \frac { d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2 I = \pi \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 } \frac { d t } { \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } \right) } {/tex} {tex}= \pi \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 }{/tex} {tex}= \pi \left[ \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \left( - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \right] {/tex} {tex}= \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex} {tex} \therefore I = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question , {tex}I =\int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { x \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex} .......(i)
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin ( \pi - x ) } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } ( \pi - x ) } d x{/tex} {tex}[\because \int _0^a f(x)= \int_0^a f(a-x)]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { ( \pi - x ) \sin x } { 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x } d x{/tex}.....(ii)
On Adding eqs.(i) and (ii),we get
{tex}2 I = \int _ { 0 } ^ { \pi } \frac { \pi \sin x } { \left( 1 + \cos ^ { 2 } x \right) } d x{/tex}
Put , {tex}cos x = t{/tex}
{tex}-sin x dx = dt{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow sinx dx = -dt{/tex}
Lower limit , when x = 0, then t = 1
Upper limit ,when  {tex}x= \pi{/tex}, then t = -1
{tex}= - \pi \int _ { 1 } ^ { - 1 } \frac { d t } { 1 + t ^ { 2 } }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow 2 I = \pi \int _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 } \frac { d t } { \left( 1 + t ^ { 2 } \right) } {/tex}
{tex}= \pi \left[ \tan ^ { - 1 } t \right] _ { - 1 } ^ { 1 }{/tex}
{tex}= \pi \left[ \frac { \pi } { 4 } - \left( - \frac { \pi } { 4 } \right) \right] {/tex}
{tex}= \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}
{tex} \therefore I = \frac { \pi ^ { 2 } } { 4 }{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let f(x) = sin 5 x.cos 4 x f(-x) = sin 5 (-x).cos 4 (-x) = -sin 5 x.cos 4 x = -f(x) {tex}\therefore{/tex} f is odd function {tex}\therefore \int\limits_{ - 1}^1 {{{\sin }^5}x.{{\cos }^4}xdx = 0} {/tex}
solution_html:
Let f(x) = sin5x.cos4x
f(-x) = sin5(-x).cos4(-x)
= -sin5x.cos4x
= -f(x)
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  f is odd function
{tex}\therefore \int\limits_{ - 1}^1 {{{\sin }^5}x.{{\cos }^4}xdx = 0} {/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_text: Evaluate {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^{4} x}{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x} d x{/tex}
solution_text: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^{4} x}{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x} d x{/tex} ...(i) Then, by using, {tex}\int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex} , we get {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^{4}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin ^{4}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos ^{4}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{4} x}{\cos ^{4} x+\sin ^{4} x} d x{/tex} ...(ii) Adding (i) and (ii), we get {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x}{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x} d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 1 \cdot d x=[x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} Hence, {tex}I=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^{4} x}{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x} d x{/tex} ...(i)
Then, by using, {tex}\int_{0}^{a} f(x) d x=\int_{0}^{a} f(a-x) d x{/tex}, we get
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^{4}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{\sin ^{4}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)+\cos ^{4}\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)} d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\cos ^{4} x}{\cos ^{4} x+\sin ^{4} x} d x{/tex} ...(ii)
Adding (i) and (ii), we get
{tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x}{\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x} d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} 1 \cdot d x=[x]_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} 
Hence, {tex}I=\frac{\pi}{4}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: {tex}I=\int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{dx}}{{1 + \sqrt {\tan x} }}} {/tex} {tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{dx}}{{1 + \sqrt {\frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}}} }} = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sqrt {\cos x} }}{{\sqrt {\cos x} + \sqrt {\sin x} }}dx} } {/tex} ...(1) {tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sqrt {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{3} + \frac{\pi }{6} - x} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{3} + \frac{\pi }{6} - x} \right)} + \sqrt {\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{3} + \frac{\pi }{6} - x} \right)} }}dx} {/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because \int_a^b {f\left( x \right)dx = \int_a^b {f\left( {a + b - x} \right)dx} } } \right]{/tex} {tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sqrt {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - x} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - x} \right)} + \sqrt {\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - x} \right)} }}dx} {/tex} {tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sqrt {\sin x} }}{{\sqrt {\sin x} + \sqrt {\cos x} }}dx} {/tex} ...(2) Adding (1) and (2), we get {tex}2I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {1dx} {/tex} {tex}= \left[ x \right]_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{\pi }{3} - \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex} {tex}I = \frac{\pi }{{12}}{/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}I=\int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{dx}}{{1 + \sqrt {\tan x} }}} {/tex}
{tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{dx}}{{1 + \sqrt {\frac{{\sin x}}{{\cos x}}} }} = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sqrt {\cos x} }}{{\sqrt {\cos x} + \sqrt {\sin x} }}dx} } {/tex}...(1)
{tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sqrt {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{3} + \frac{\pi }{6} - x} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{3} + \frac{\pi }{6} - x} \right)} + \sqrt {\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{3} + \frac{\pi }{6} - x} \right)} }}dx} {/tex}
{tex}\left[ {\because \int_a^b {f\left( x \right)dx = \int_a^b {f\left( {a + b - x} \right)dx} } } \right]{/tex}
{tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sqrt {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - x} \right)} }}{{\sqrt {\cos \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - x} \right)} + \sqrt {\sin \left( {\frac{\pi }{2} - x} \right)} }}dx} {/tex}
{tex}I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {\frac{{\sqrt {\sin x} }}{{\sqrt {\sin x} + \sqrt {\cos x} }}dx} {/tex} ...(2)
Adding (1) and (2), we get
{tex}2I = \int_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3} {1dx} {/tex}
{tex}= \left[ x \right]_{\pi /6}^{\pi /3}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac{\pi }{3} - \frac{\pi }{6} = \frac{\pi }{6}{/tex}
{tex}I = \frac{\pi }{{12}}{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
solution_text: Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin x d x{/tex} {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \cos x d x{/tex} Adding the two values of I, we get {tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\log \sin x+\log \cos x) d x{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\log \sin x \cos x+\log 2-\log 2) d x{/tex} (by adding and subtracting log 2) = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin 2 x d x-\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log 2 d x{/tex} Put 2x = t in the first integral. Then 2 dx = dt, when x = 0, t = 0 and when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} , t = {tex}\pi{/tex} Therefore, 2I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin t d t-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2}{2} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin t d t-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} = {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin x d x-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} ((by changing variable t to x) = {tex}I-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} Hence, {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin x d x=\frac{-\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}
solution_html:
Let {tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin x d x{/tex} 
{tex}I=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right) d x=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \cos x d x{/tex} 
Adding the two values of I, we get
{tex}2 \mathrm{I}=\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\log \sin x+\log \cos x) d x{/tex} 
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}(\log \sin x \cos x+\log 2-\log 2) d x{/tex} (by adding and subtracting log 2)
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin 2 x d x-\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log 2 d x{/tex} 
Put 2x = t in the first integral. Then 2 dx = dt, when x = 0, t = 0 and when {tex}x=\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}, t = {tex}\pi{/tex} 
Therefore, 2I = {tex}\frac{1}{2} \int_{0}^{\pi} \log \sin t d t-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{2}{2} \int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin t d t-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}  
= {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin x d x-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} ((by changing variable t to x)
= {tex}I-\frac{\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex} 
Hence, {tex}\int_{0}^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \log \sin x d x=\frac{-\pi}{2} \log 2{/tex}

solution_text: Put t = 1 + sin 6x, so that dt = 6 cos 6x dx Therefore {tex}\int \cos 6 x \sqrt{1+\sin 6 x} d x{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \int t^{\frac{1}{2}} d t{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{2}{3}(t)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\mathrm{C}=\frac{1}{9}(1+\sin 6 x)^{\frac{3}{2}}+\mathrm{C}{/tex}
